children.summary: Separate an argument into premises offered as reasons and the conclusion they support.
children.title: 1.1 Arguments

children.summary: Distinguish whether reasoning follows from whether its premises happen to be true.
children.title: 1.2 The Scope of Logic

children.summary: Relate validity, consistency, equivalence, and contradiction without treating them as synonyms.
children.title: 1.3 Other Logical Notions

children.summary: Review how premises support conclusions and how validity differs from truth, consistency, and contradiction.
children.title: 1.8 Review

children.summary: Analyze a short argument, identify its structure, and classify the logical relationship among its claims.
children.title: 1.9 Checkpoint

rootNodes.summary: Review how premises support conclusions and how validity differs from truth, consistency, and contradiction.
rootNodes.title: 1.0 Logic, Arguments, and Meaning

children.summary: Expose an argument pattern by replacing complete atomic claims with stable sentence letters.
children.title: 2.1 First Steps to Symbolization

children.summary: Translate negation, conjunction, disjunction, conditional, and biconditional structure into TFL.
children.title: 2.2 Connectives

children.summary: Recognize well-formed TFL sentences and identify their main connective.
children.title: 2.3 Sentences of TFL

children.summary: Resolve lexical or structural ambiguity before committing to one formalization.
children.title: 2.4 Ambiguity

children.summary: Distinguish using an expression to talk about the world from mentioning the expression itself.
children.title: 2.5 Use and Mention

children.summary: Translate ordinary claims into well-formed TFL while preserving repeated atoms, grouping, and the use–mention distinction.
children.title: 2.8 Review

children.summary: Disambiguate a short passage, give its symbolization key and TFL form, and identify the main connective.
children.title: 2.9 Checkpoint

rootNodes.summary: Translate ordinary claims into well-formed TFL while preserving repeated atoms, grouping, and the use–mention distinction.
rootNodes.title: 2.0 Truth-Functional Language

children.summary: Evaluate each TFL connective from the truth values of its immediate components.
children.title: 3.1 Characteristic Truth Tables

children.summary: Determine whether a connective’s output is fixed entirely by its component truth values.
children.title: 3.2 Truth-Functional Connectives

children.summary: Construct every valuation for a formula and evaluate it from the inside out.
children.title: 3.3 Complete Truth Tables

children.summary: Use truth tables to test tautology, contradiction, equivalence, satisfiability, entailment, and validity.
children.title: 3.4 Semantic Concepts

children.summary: Evaluate TFL formulas systematically and connect complete truth-table patterns to semantic classifications.
children.title: 3.8 Review

children.summary: Build or inspect a truth table, identify a decisive row, and justify a semantic verdict.
children.title: 3.9 Checkpoint

rootNodes.summary: Evaluate TFL formulas systematically and connect complete truth-table patterns to semantic classifications.
rootNodes.title: 3.0 Truth Tables

children.summary: Recognize valid reasoning that TFL cannot reveal because atomic sentence structure has been hidden.
children.title: 4.1 Limitations of TFL

children.summary: Skip unnecessary component calculations while preserving a justified semantic test.
children.title: 4.2 Truth Table Shortcuts

children.summary: Construct a decisive valuation instead of a full table when one counterexample is enough.
children.title: 4.3 Partial Truth Tables

children.summary: Choose between complete and partial truth-table methods while respecting what TFL can and cannot represent.
children.title: 4.8 Review

children.summary: Find and explain a decisive valuation, or explain why TFL is too coarse to capture the original inference.
children.title: 4.9 Checkpoint

rootNodes.summary: Choose between complete and partial truth-table methods while respecting what TFL can and cannot represent.
rootNodes.title: 4.0 Semantic Reasoning in TFL

children.summary: Explain how a derivation displays a chain of licensed inference rather than checking every valuation.
children.title: 5.1 The Idea of Natural Deduction

children.summary: Select introduction or elimination rules based on the main connective of available formulas and goals.
children.title: 5.2 Basic Rules for TFL

children.summary: Plan a proof by working backward from the goal and forward from usable premises.
children.title: 5.3 Constructing Proofs

children.summary: Match proof goals and available formulas to introduction, elimination, and subproof strategies.
children.title: 5.8 Review

children.summary: Plan and explain a short derivation, including every assumption, rule choice, and discharged subproof.
children.title: 5.9 Checkpoint

rootNodes.summary: Match proof goals and available formulas to introduction, elimination, and subproof strategies.
rootNodes.title: 5.0 Natural Deduction Foundations

children.summary: Apply convenient rules such as DS, MT, DNE, excluded middle, and De Morgan transformations with exact premises.
children.title: 6.1 Additional Rules for TFL

children.summary: Read the single turnstile as a claim about derivability and distinguish it from semantic entailment.
children.title: 6.2 Proof-Theoretic Concepts

children.summary: Expand a convenient derived-rule step into a proof using only the basic rules.
children.title: 6.3 Derived Rules

children.summary: Relate derivability and semantic consequence through the soundness and completeness guarantees.
children.title: 6.4 Soundness and Completeness

children.summary: Use additional and derived rules accurately while keeping proof-theoretic and semantic claims distinct.
children.title: 6.8 Review

children.summary: Analyze a short proof strategy and explain how soundness, completeness, or derived-rule expansion justifies it.
children.title: 6.9 Checkpoint

rootNodes.summary: Use additional and derived rules accurately while keeping proof-theoretic and semantic claims distinct.
rootNodes.title: 6.0 Proof Strategy

children.summary: Distinguish names, predicates, variables, quantifiers, and the domain when decomposing an atomic claim.
children.title: 7.1 Building Blocks of FOL

children.summary: Choose a domain and symbolize common one-quantifier claims with the correct connective and scope.
children.title: 7.2 Sentences with One Quantifier

children.summary: Track predicate-place order and quantifier order in claims about several objects.
children.title: 7.3 Multiple Generality

children.summary: Use identity and non-identity to express sameness, exceptions, and finite numerical claims.
children.title: 7.4 Identity

children.summary: Retrieve how domains, predicate places, quantifier scope, and identity determine a first-order symbolization.
children.title: 7.8 Review

children.summary: Build and explain a finite first-order symbolization key, then compare formulas that differ in quantifier order or identity.
children.title: 7.9 Checkpoint

rootNodes.summary: Retrieve how domains, predicate places, quantifier scope, and identity determine a first-order symbolization.
rootNodes.title: 7.0 First-Order Language

children.summary: Recognize FOL terms, formulas, sentences, bound variables, and free variables by the formation rules.
children.title: 8.1 Sentences of FOL

children.summary: Analyze “the F is G” as existence, uniqueness, and predication without inventing a name.
children.title: 8.2 Definite Descriptions

children.summary: Separate scope readings of ordinary-language claims before selecting a first-order formula.
children.title: 8.3 Ambiguity

children.summary: Review well-formed FOL sentences, Russell-style descriptions, and the scope choices that distinguish competing readings.
children.title: 8.8 Review

children.summary: Diagnose a first-order expression for free variables, uniqueness, and scope, then defend one explicit reading of an ambiguous claim.
children.title: 8.9 Checkpoint

rootNodes.summary: Review well-formed FOL sentences, Russell-style descriptions, and the scope choices that distinguish competing readings.
rootNodes.title: 8.0 Expressing First-Order Claims

children.summary: Specify predicate extensions and explain why FOL cannot distinguish predicates with the same extension merely by differences in meaning.
children.title: 9.1 Extensionality

children.summary: Evaluate atomic, truth-functional, and quantified FOL sentences in a finite interpretation.
children.title: 9.2 Truth in FOL

children.summary: Classify validity, contradiction, equivalence, entailment, and joint satisfiability across interpretations.
children.title: 9.3 Semantic Concepts

children.summary: Retrieve how domains, referents, extensions, satisfaction, and interpretation-wide definitions determine FOL semantics.
children.title: 9.8 Review

children.summary: Evaluate a finite interpretation and use it to classify a semantic claim with an explicit witness or counterexample.
children.title: 9.9 Checkpoint

rootNodes.summary: Retrieve how domains, referents, extensions, satisfaction, and interpretation-wide definitions determine FOL semantics.
rootNodes.title: 9.0 Interpretations

children.summary: Construct a small counter-interpretation to refute a proposed validity, entailment, equivalence, or contradiction.
children.title: 10.1 Using Interpretations

children.summary: Distinguish claims settled by one interpretation from claims that require reasoning about all interpretations.
children.title: 10.2 Reasoning About Interpretations

children.summary: Recognize and formalize reflexive, symmetric, transitive, and antisymmetric relation properties.
children.title: 10.3 Properties of Relations

children.summary: Retrieve how countermodels, all-interpretation arguments, and named relation properties support semantic reasoning.
children.title: 10.8 Review

children.summary: Construct or diagnose a finite interpretation, then state exactly which semantic claim and relation properties it establishes.
children.title: 10.9 Checkpoint

rootNodes.summary: Retrieve how countermodels, all-interpretation arguments, and named relation properties support semantic reasoning.
rootNodes.title: 10.0 Relational Reasoning

children.summary: Apply universal and existential introduction and elimination while checking substitution and fresh-name restrictions.
children.title: 11.1 Basic Rules for FOL

children.summary: Order forward and backward proof moves so quantifier restrictions and subproof goals are satisfied.
children.title: 11.2 Proofs with Quantifiers

children.summary: Move negation across quantifiers with the correct quantifier switch and justify the conversion.
children.title: 11.3 Conversion of Quantifiers

children.summary: Use identity introduction for reflexivity and identity elimination to substitute co-referring names in a controlled proof step.
children.title: 11.4 Rules for Identity

children.summary: Retrieve quantifier-rule restrictions, proof-order strategy, conversion laws, and identity substitution without treating names as arbitrary by default.
children.title: 11.8 Review

children.summary: Construct or diagnose a quantified proof, citing each line and checking fresh-name, scope, subproof, and identity-substitution conditions.
children.title: 11.9 Checkpoint

rootNodes.summary: Retrieve quantifier-rule restrictions, proof-order strategy, conversion laws, and identity substitution without treating names as arbitrary by default.
rootNodes.title: 11.0 Quantifier and Identity Proofs

children.summary: Justify quantifier-conversion shortcuts by expanding them into basic-rule derivations.
children.title: 12.1 Derived Rules

children.summary: Choose a proof or an interpretation as the efficient witness for a positive or negative logical claim while keeping turnstiles distinct.
children.title: 12.2 Proofs and Semantics

children.summary: Recognize and construct equivalent disjunctive and conjunctive normal forms from decisive truth-table rows.
children.title: 12.3 Normal Forms

children.summary: Explain functional completeness and test whether a proposed connective basis can express every truth function.
children.title: 12.4 Functional Completeness

children.summary: Build a checkable chain of equivalence-preserving replacements and use it to reach a requested normal form.
children.title: 12.5 Proving Equivalences

children.summary: Connect derived quantifier rules, proof-semantic certificates, normal forms, functional completeness, and equivalence chains into one auditable toolkit.
children.title: 12.8 Review

children.summary: Choose and complete a proof, countermodel, normal-form construction, or equivalence chain, then audit every dependency and transformation.
children.title: 12.9 Checkpoint

rootNodes.summary: Connect derived quantifier rules, proof-semantic certificates, normal forms, functional completeness, and equivalence chains into one auditable toolkit.
rootNodes.title: 12.0 Proof Mastery

courses.summary: Twelve modules from argument analysis through first-order proof and formal equivalence.
courses.title: Logic Foundations
